text:
holy fuck I just solved the US debt problem including rising bond yields….

drop the traditional economic rulebook and look at this through a highly speculative, macro-engineering lens. 

To make a sovereign-level, asset-backed bond system actually work, we have to look past individual graphics chips and treat the entire tech, space, and AI sector as a unified, hyper-valuable physical engine.
If we extrapolate this concept to the scale of the U.S. government, here is how a "Next-Gen Infrastructure Backed Bond" system could actually work.

1. The Asset Base: From "Chips" to "Compute & Orbit Capitals"
To solve the scale problem, the government wouldn't back bonds with loose hardware; it would back them with sovereign computing grids and orbital real estate.
•The SpaceX Network Multiplier: SpaceX's valuation isn't just about rockets; it is about Starlink and the monopolization of low-Earth orbit (LEO). If the U.S. government nationalizes or builds a massive equity-share model in SpaceX and megacluster data centers, the collateral becomes the global routing infrastructure of the future.
•Energy-Compute Monopolies: The government links these bonds to federal land containing nuclear-powered AI megastructures. The asset is no longer a depreciating chip, but the perpetual monopoly on AI intelligence and global satellite bandwidth.

2. Rewriting Obsolescence: The "Compute Standard"

Instead of backing a bond with a specific Nvidia Blackwell or Rubin chip, the U.S. Treasury establishes a "Sovereign Flop" or "Compute Standard" (similar to how the U.S. used the Gold Standard).
•The Mechanism: The bond guarantees the holder a fixed percentage of the nation's total computing throughput or data-routing revenues over 30 years.
•The Upgrade Loop: As tech upgrades from silicon photonics to quantum computing, the physical hardware changes, but the bond's claim on the computational power remains constant. The value of the sector doesn't rot; it grows exponentially as efficiency increases.

3. Hyper-Inflation of Sector Value
To match a $34+ trillion national debt, the value of the tech/space sector must be artificially and structurally inflated. The government could achieve this through forced tokenization and regulatory moats:
•Tokenized Infrastructure: Every satellite launch, every AI API call, and every terabyte of space data gets routed through a sovereign blockchain.
•Global Dependency: The U.S. forces allied nations to settle international data and defense contracts using these compute-backed instruments. This instantly drives the sector's valuation from a few trillion dollars to tens of trillions, effectively matching the scale of the U.S. Treasury market.

What This Future Looks Like
If the U.S. government executed this, the traditional Treasury market would merge with Silicon Valley and aerospace:
•The "Risk-Free Rate" would no longer be based on the Federal Reserve's interest rate, but on the aggregate processing power of the American AI grid and Starshield defense network.
•Investors wouldn't buy U.S. debt to get cash back; they would buy U.S. debt to secure the data rights and processing power needed to run their own corporations and autonomous industries.

text:
Here’s a new long idea: $LWLG 

I keep coming back to $LWLG that recently ran from $3 to $18 and then back to $9.

Every optical interconnect roadmap past 1.6T hits the same wall: electronics can’t switch light fast enough without burning power nobody can afford to spend at hyperscale.

Hyperscalers are already running into thermal and power ceilings inside the optical layer, not just the GPU layer. The fix everyone’s chasing is a modulator that converts electrical signal to optical signal with less power loss than silicon photonics currently allows.

Lightwave Logic has spent over a decade building exactly that. Its electro-optic polymer platform, branded P2IC, uses proprietary organic polymers instead of silicon to build modulators that move data faster while drawing less power per bit.

The irreplaceability case is real but early. Competing approaches exist in lithium niobate and silicon photonics from much larger players, but none have commercialized a polymer-based modulator at production scale yet. If LWLG’s tech clears volume manufacturing first, the qualification cycle for hyperscaler customers locks in multi-year exclusivity, because nobody requalifies an optical interconnect supplier casually.

Financially, this is still a development-stage story. Revenue is in the low single-digit millions. The stock trades on a roadmap, not a P&L, which is the honest read.

Catalysts: continued progress on Stage 3 customer prototypes and foundry device deliveries (expected H2 2026), followed by potential material supply/licensing agreements. First meaningful production-volume revenue targeted for 2027 per management guidance. A strategic partnership announcement with a transceiver or hyperscaler-adjacent customer. Continued capital raises as the company scales toward manufacturing, which cuts both ways.

Bear case, stated plainly. This has been a “next year is the year” story for a long time. If a production-scale order doesn’t materialize in 2026, the stock re-rates down hard, because there’s no earnings floor to catch it. Watch cash burn rate and any further dilutive raises as the two early warning signals.

Market cap $1.5B.
TAM: $47B.
SAM: $2-4B.

Early lead in foundry PDKs and customer prototypes (4+ Fortune Global 500 at Stage 3)

TLDR:

> Sole-ish commercial path to polymer-based optical modulators at L4

> $1.5B market cap, pre-revenue-scale, roadmap-driven valuation

> Catalyst window: production-volume order disclosure in the next 2-4 quarters

> Bear case: no order, no earnings floor, the stock has nowhere to hide

-BP

Please note: This is not financial advice.

text:
Most people are still fixated on the obvious AI names.  The more interesting setups right now are sitting quietly in narrower parts:

• Advanced packaging materials (underfill & next-gen substrates) — increasingly critical for chiplets, yet supply is tightly held.

• Silicon Photonics — positioned to scale optical connectivity more affordably than traditional approaches, with integration challenges still ahead.

• Specialty chemicals & films for high-end packaging — long qualification timelines create real stickiness for qualified suppliers.

• High-speed electro-optical test equipment — quietly becoming a constraint as data rates push higher.These areas carry structural bottlenecks but haven’t been as widely chased yet.

I’m keeping an eye on qualification progress and capacity signals here.

text:
How do Photonics, Fiber Optics, and Networking power the AI boom? 🧵👇

Think of an AI data center as a high-speed highway system built for massive data workloads:

⚡ Photonics (The Cars): The technology using lasers instead of copper wires to move data. It eliminates heat and allows AI processors (GPUs) to communicate instantly using light.

🛤️ Fiber Optics (The Highway): The physical glass cables that carry those light signals. They act as the zero-loss pipeline moving massive bandwidth between servers.

🚦 Networking (The Traffic Control): The overarching system of switches and protocols (like InfiniBand). It connects thousands of GPUs together, routing data traffic so they can train models as one giant brain.

TL;DR: Photonics creates the light, Fiber Optics carries it, and Networking directs it. 🚀

text:
Every stock looks obvious in hindsight.

Nobody gets rewarded for buying after the thesis is universally accepted.

The biggest winners are often the stocks that look boring, misunderstood, or outright wrong at the start.

- People laughed at semicap before AI.
- People ignored photonics before bandwidth became the bottleneck.
- People dismissed power semis before power efficiency became the bottleneck.

Today, $XFAB gets labeled as "just another automotive foundry."

Market is once again underestimating where the next bottleneck is forming: power, SiC, sensors, and specialty manufacturing.

We'll find out soon enough.

Markets don't care about consensus.

user_name: Dora＠投資テーマを、漫画で学ぶ
text:
【速報📡】Coherent（$COHR）技術速報 NVIDIAは”光の供給網”を資本で押さえた

👇漫画で図解

不足している”光部材”を、NVIDIAが出資と購入契約で先回り確保。

■何が起きた？🔍 💰 NVIDIA→Coherentへ20億ドル出資 📦 数十億ドル規模の購入コミット 🏭 テキサス州Sherman・InP工場を拡張着工 ・6インチInP製造スペース 2倍 ・ウェハ生産能力 4倍

■政策支援も重なる🏛️ 🇺🇸 CHIPS法 直接支援 最大5,000万ドル（LOI段階） ⭐ テキサス州・Sherman市 先行支援 約2,000万ドル

■InPから何が生まれる？💡 🔆 レーザー（EML/CW） 📡 光トランシーバ 🔗 CPO/光集積部品 → Spectrum-X / Quantum-X Photonics関連へ直結
■本質はここ🎯 光不足の話ではない。 最大需要家NVIDIAが「出資＋購入契約＋政策支援」の組み合わせで供給網を先回り確保した構造変化。

■投資家チェックポイント👀 📅 量産立ち上がり時期 📊 購入コミットの売上認識タイミング 💹 粗利率・利益化の進捗
■結論⚖️ 光のトレードは「誰が足りないか」から「誰が資本で押さえたか」へ。 COHRには追い風だが、良いニュースと今買えるかは別。

※CHIPS支援はLOI段階 / 本投稿は投資助言ではなく、教育目的の解説です

text:
https://t.co/V0vE8tbm8V

Our work provides a valuable design strategy for realizing  multifunctional, multiband, highly efficient, and dynamically tunable  polarization manipulation devices, which is expected to advance the  development of polarization devices and chiral functional devices in the  field of integrated photonics.

Taiwan IC design companies are jumping into the optical communication boom. 📡

MediaTek is leading the way, along with its subsidiaries Dafu and Yuancheng, plus Elan. All are working on silicon photonics and CPO technology for next-generation AI platforms like Nvidia’s Vera Rubin.

MediaTek is using its high-speed tech to develop 400Gbps CPO and low-power Micro LED solutions.  Also investing in Ayar Labs.

Dafu expects its optical communication revenue to triple this year (mainly from PAM4 DSP chips for modules).

Yuancheng is developing silicon photonics chips and engines.

Elan is partnering and investing in silicon photonics too.

This is a smart move because AI data centers need faster and more efficient optical connections.

The optical side of the AI supply chain is heating up quickly.

text:
So another big fish is betting on the most revolutionary technology that $SIVE benefits from

One of many that it benefits from
Samsung Electronics is accelerating the development of CPO technology

Aiming to launch it in 2027

With its sights set on challenging $TSMC dominance in the semiconductor foundry sector through silicon photonics

This innovation, based on SiPh and CPO
Allows electrical signals to be converted into optical signals to transmit data at terabit per second speeds

In doing so, it efficiently solves the main bottlenecks in AI

Limited bandwidth, excess heat, and high energy consumption

With this strategy, Samsung seeks to compete directly against the powerhouse alliance of $NVDA $AVGO and $TSMC

To achieve this, it is attracting specialized talent from $INTC and expanding its R&D centers

Positioning CPO as a potential game changer in advanced chip packaging

Is anyone going to stop giving $SIVE more reasons and catalysts to pull a 20x from here?

How do you even dare to be short on this hahaha

Makes absolutely no sense

$JBL is developing a 1.6T LRO pluggable optical transceiver using $SIVE DFB lasers.

Jabil management later described the LRO as having potential for a “relatively dramatic moat”.

The map now looks like this:

$SIVE → $JBL 1.6T pluggables
$SIVE → $GFS silicon photonics reference designs / SCALE
$SIVE → O-Net + Enablence external light sources
$SIVE → $POET optical interposer / ELS modules
$SIVE → Ayar Labs SuperNova light source

Then the OSINT map gets interesting as well:
possible adjacency to Marvell/Celestial, Lightmatter, Lightelligence and other optical-I/O players.

The thesis is not “one design win”.
The thesis is that Sivers might be the laser layer repeatedly designed into the architectures AI infrastructure needs next.

Pluggables.
CPO.
External light sources.
Optical engines.
Foundry reference designs.

Pluggable, scale out CPO, scale up CPO, NPO, etc. - Sivers and $JBL went god-mode and developed 1.6T optical transceivers with CW lasers. Effectively designing around the EML bottlenecks, and even made things more power efficient. From quotes from Jabil management they created a “relatively dramatic moat”. So for the next gen of 1.6T pluggable transceivers, Sivers seems immediately used. Markets missed this nuance too: after Jabil’s announcement, other pluggable players reached out, and SIVE is working with them now (prob codevelopment, qualification stage). There’s not too many players that build optical transceivers that aren’t vertically integrated like Lumentum (think: Innolight/eoptolink, and maybe others). So these are active developments, just not public material yet and could be a new press release anytime. - for CPO scale out which happens H2 2026 onward like $POET, $SIVE is a laser supplier for those players. - scale up CPO is h2 2027, from Ayar and Nvidia’a NVLink CPO ecosystem and that’s the main volume ramp across optical players. But this is where $SIVE looks like they mog every other player from supply chain mapping. Since they’ve likely been working + designed in with $MRVL Celestial, Lightmatter, Lightelligence and the others since they were small way back when. On top of things like this: For foundries like $GFS, $SIVE is the reference laser. Not even including massive companies like O-Net building ELS with $SIVE, likely for the Asian hyperscalers supply chains. This is why $SIVE is by far my favorite laser chokepoint long. Then $MU $SNDK etc. went to Saturn.

Now AI Agents can be the "matrix multiplication" equivalents for Networking:

"A single agentic task can generate 450% more network traffic than a human doing the same work" 👀🔥🔥

Imagine the networking traffic 🚀 And in AI, Networking ==> Photonics & Glass $LITE $AAOI $NOK  $GLW

A single agentic task can generate 450% more network traffic than a human doing the same work. Roughly 70% of that traffic is inference. Nearly 10% of AI flows now carry more data upstream than downstream, versus 0.5% for typical web traffic. And Cisco says enterprise traffic could grow 9x by 2035 with agentic AI adoption, versus 2.5x without it. That ties directly into something I wrote about recently: Scale-across. AI clusters are getting too large to keep in one place. At a certain point, the constraint becomes physical: Power. Land. Cooling. Permitting. Fiber access. Route availability. So instead of forcing everything into one giant site, hyperscalers start connecting multiple buildings, campuses, and facilities together so they can operate like one larger AI environment. Cisco’s report adds the usage layer. Agentic AI turns one request into a chain: Prompt → planner → model call → tool call → retrieval → more model calls → action. That creates more: > traffic > upstream flow. > machine-to-machine exchange > persistent context movement > pressure on the infrastructure underneath the model. The first pressure shows up closest to compute. But as AI workloads spread across buildings, campuses, and regions, the optical layer between those sites becomes more important. That is the scale-across trade. Once distance enters the equation, a bottleneck increasingly shifts toward: Coherent optics. Fiber. DCI. Dark fiber routes. Pump lasers. Narrow-linewidth lasers. Optical amplification. Indium phosphide capacity. That is why this keeps showing up across optical earnings calls. $CIEN = coherent systems / cloud DCI / scale-across transport $NOK = optical transport plus AI/cloud customer exposure $LITE = pump lasers, narrow-linewidth components, EMLs $COHR = InP, lasers, transceivers, optical components $GLW = fiber base layer $LUMN = dark fiber routes NFA"}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2062396054449144251"}}, "rest_id": "2067301749283766756", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "22320", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2067347493356966120", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3998", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
20260617 Market Review
• I don't to talk to you about the FOMC today. You can get that from tons of other website, talking heads etc. Most of these have no value add and not actionable. What's important is HOW do we interpret what is going and HOW do we position going forward? ie HOW DO WE THINK AHEAD OF THE CROWD?
• Market breadth ended up being quite weak, with the VIX threatening to go back above 19 (a line in the sand I watch). But what really caught my eye was the USD. It really took off after the FOMC and stopped right at resistance. If this leads to a sustained breakout to the upside, it may not be friendly to markets. Watching that 100.50 level closely. With the USD rallying, it is no wonder $GOLD would get hit. Another lower high forming. 
• Indices seem to be stuck in chop for now. AI seems to be the only game in town but I wanna dig deeper into the various subsectors today. 
• Semiconductors. Ok but hitting resistance. 
$NVDA losing the 50SMA today is not a good look. 
• Semicon Equipment and Materials. Big list in here, very mixed picture. Nothing I would buy in a hurry.
• Racks: $Dell ok but $HPE selling off. Neutral because the whole group is not looking uniform. 
• Photonics: $LITE is the group leader and it has been mucking around the 50SMA. Not healthy. Neither is $AAOI, $GLW or $AXTI. 
• Memory: $STX and $WDC still making new highs, but $MU hitting resistance. $SNDK still king. Of all the AI Hardware playes, Memory is probably the only subsector which I think overall is still healthy. But even that is extended. 
• So the above are the AI Leading Sectors that I pay attention to and overall, my conclusion is that the easy trade in AI Hardware is over. It can still go higher, but unless it sets up for me properly (with a pullback or an orderly sideways congestion for a few weeks to a few months), I will find it hard to bet big on them for now. 
• I am still very bullish on the AI trade but if Hardware is not the game for now, what then?
• Next we have the Neoclouds. $IREN $CRWV $NBIS etc. $NBIS is the clear leader. Generally ok and I would still look for opportunities in them.
• Software. I was hopeful that it might have pulled back too much and would find support but that didn't eventuate. In line with my trading philosophy, "if it ain't working, it ain't staying". I will continue to stalk this sector as they are going to be the "operational" layer of AI and I think there will be several companies in there that will benefit. 
• Cybersecurity is one software sub-sector I am super keen on and have taken a position accordingly. Look at $CRWD and $PANW (both we took in our port today) for example. They are holding up like a champ and tightening up really nicely. This is an extremely healthy consolidation after that fierce run they had since early May, and the best part is, they are NOT giving back much of the gains! THIS is a sign of a Market Leader. Can we be wrong or too early? Of course, but then that’s why we have stops to protect us. 
• Biotech. This is the sector that I think will benefit greatly from AI (check out the $NVDA x $LLY presentation on the NVDA website). I have been stalking XBI for a few weeks now and we finally broke higher today. Great volume too. 
• Fintech is another interesting subsector that is showing a bit of relative strength recently. Worth keeping an eye on. Eg $DAVE $AFRM etc.
• In conclusion, I think the overall market might be in for a bit of digestion. $MAGS  is pulling back hard (not good). The AI HARDWARE trade is no longer easy. But the AI USAGE trade I think is just starting. Biotech and Cybersecurity $CIBR are the standouts to me and we are positioning accordingly. Software $IGV is a maybe, keep stalking.
• All the best.

You can get that from tons of other website, talking heads etc. Most of these have no value add and not actionable. What's important is HOW do we interpret what is going and HOW do we position going forward? ie HOW DO WE THINK AHEAD OF THE CROWD? • Market breadth ended up being quite weak, with the VIX threatening to go back above 19 (a line in the sand I watch). But what really caught my eye was the USD. It really took off after the FOMC and stopped right at resistance. If this leads to a sustained breakout to the upside, it may not be friendly to markets. Watching that 100.50 level closely. With the USD rallying, it is no wonder $GOLD would get hit. Another lower high forming. • Indices seem to be stuck in chop for now. AI seems to be the only game in town but I wanna dig deeper into the various subsectors today. • Semiconductors. Ok but hitting resistance. $NVDA losing the 50SMA today is not a good look. • Semicon Equipment and Materials. Big list in here, very mixed picture. Nothing I would buy in a hurry. • Racks: $Dell ok but $HPE selling off. Neutral because the whole group is not looking uniform. • Photonics: $LITE is the group leader and it has been mucking around the 50SMA. Not healthy. Neither is $AAOI, $GLW or $AXTI. • Memory: $STX and $WDC still making new highs, but $MU hitting resistance. $SNDK still king. Of all the AI Hardware playes, Memory is probably the only subsector which I think overall is still healthy. But even that is extended. • So the above are the AI Leading Sectors that I pay attention to and overall, my conclusion is that the easy trade in AI Hardware is over. It can still go higher, but unless it sets up for me properly (with a pullback or an orderly sideways congestion for a few weeks to a few months), I will find it hard to bet big on them for now. • I am still very bullish on the AI trade but if Hardware is not the game for now, what then? • Next we have the Neoclouds. $IREN $CRWV $NBIS etc. $NBIS is the clear leader. Generally ok and I would still look for opportunities in them. • Software. I was hopeful that it might have pulled back too much and would find support but that didn't eventuate. In line with my trading philosophy, "if it ain't working, it ain't staying". I will continue to stalk this sector as they are going to be the "operational" layer of AI and I think there will be several companies in there that will benefit. • Cybersecurity is one software sub-sector I am super keen on and have taken a position accordingly. Look at $CRWD and $PANW (both we took in our port today) for example. They are holding up like a champ and tightening up really nicely. This is an extremely healthy consolidation after that fierce run they had since early May, and the best part is, they are NOT giving back much of the gains! THIS is a sign of a Market Leader. Can we be wrong or too early? Of course, but then that’s why we have stops to protect us. • Biotech. This is the sector that I think will benefit greatly from AI (check out the $NVDA x $LLY presentation on the NVDA website). I have been stalking XBI for a few weeks now and we finally broke higher today. Great volume too. • Fintech is another interesting subsector that is showing a bit of relative strength recently. Worth keeping an eye on. Eg $DAVE $AFRM etc. • In conclusion, I think the overall market might be in for a bit of digestion. $MAGS is pulling back hard (not good). The AI HARDWARE trade is no longer easy. But the AI USAGE trade I think is just starting. Biotech and Cybersecurity $CIBR are the standouts to me and we are positioning accordingly. Software $IGV is a maybe, keep stalking. • All the best."}}}, "rest_id": "2067347221503471760", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "191", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: Cleanest read of the quarter, and agreed it's a genuine, gimmick-free beat. The one thing I'd push on: this is a high-quality revenue print, not yet a high-quality margin one. Intelligent Infrastructure is carrying the AI growth (21%, guided +32% into Q4) but sits at 6.1% core margin, only ~30bps above the 5.8% corporate blend. So the AI surge is roughly margin-neutral to the company today. The whole re-rate toward 6%+ is a within-II mix story (photonics, cooling, power, Hanley) that management itself calls gradual. Volume is here now; the accretive mix is FY27's job. That's the gap between "beat-and-raise" and "re-rate," and it's why a 10bps full-year margin lift on a $1B revenue raise is the number to sit with.

text:
$SIVE has had a tremendous run, but at current levels it looks increasingly priced for perfection.

Meanwhile, $LBIRD remains largely overlooked despite being directly exposed to one of the fastest-growing trends in aerospace: satellite constellations and optical communications.

As SpaceX, Starlink and the broader space economy continue to expand, Lumibird's laser and photonics expertise could become significantly more valuable.

Sometimes the best opportunities are where the market isn't looking yet. #SpaceX #Starlink #Photonics #Space

text:
The best-performing thing in the AI trade this year is not the GPU, the model, or the memory
-> it is a wafer most investors have never heard of, can't pronounce, and would struggle to explain. It ran over 1000% in one of my books. Here is what it actually is.

It is the substrate. 
The crystal wafer a laser gets grown on, sitting four layers upstream of the chip everyone watches. And the reason I trust the signal is that it did not turn up in one book. It turned up in two.

I run a US bottleneck book and a separate EU one. No shared holdings, different construction, different markets. This year the strongest layer in both was the same one almost nobody can name. Not the accelerator. Not even the laser. The wafer the laser is grown on.

Look at the leaderboards side by side.

In the US book, InP and Substrates is the second-strongest basket of the year, behind only memory. In the EU book, InP and Substrates is also second, behind only photonics. Same layer, same rank, two markets that do not coordinate.

The names tell it cleaner:

- US -> $AXTI +475% YTD
- EU -> $IQE.L +1018%, $SOI.PA (Soitec) +438%
and $AIXA.DE (AIXTRON) +247% selling the reactors that grow the wafers

The mechanism is the photonics build. Co-packaged optics needs lasers. Lasers are III-V material, indium phosphide and gallium arsenide, not plain silicon. III-V starts as an epitaxial wafer grown on a specialty substrate, and that supply is small, slow, and hard to add. When optical demand turned from slideware into orders, the squeeze hit the substrate first. Both books found the same constrained object without talking to each other.

Worth the honesty: these are thin floats, so the moves are violent and part momentum. Strip the two biggest and the picture cools. But the substrate is the real upstream gate on the optics bottleneck everyone keeps drawing one layer too high.

The feed watches the photonic switch. The squeeze is one floor below it, in the wafer that switch can't exist without.

text:
⚛️ Atom Computing and Nu Quantum have signed an MoU to integrate neutral-atom quantum computers with state-of-the-art photonic networking hardware.

🤝 This collaboration is set to accelerate the path to utility-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computing systems.

👀 By focusing on integrated photonics switches and distributed architectures, they're addressing key challenges on the road to real-world quantum utility.

🚀 Excited to see where this partnership leads the industry!

Get it here 👉 https://t.co/QcBJzJz04F

#QuantumComputing #AtomComputing #NuQuantum #QuantumNetworking #Science #QuantumScience @Atom_Computing @NuQuantumLtd

text:
Most people see $NOK as sleepy European telecom. They’re missing what it bought.

Less than 2% of global semiconductor advanced test & packaging happens in the US. Nokia owns one of the only domestic sites packaging photonic chips into optical modules for AI data centers — and it didn’t come from telecom. It came from Infinera, absorbed Feb 2025.

Now they’re scaling it with CHIPS money behind them:

~$30M Nokia capex + ~$10M federal CHIPS tax credit + ~$4M PA aid. 500+ jobs. Part of a $4B US commitment.

This isn’t a photonics rocket — Allentown is a small piece of a $20B+ company. But that’s the point. You’re getting a CHIPS-backed domestic photonics packaging franchise inside a name the market still prices as legacy telecom.

A misread legacy company with a real foothold in the AI photonics layer. The choke point before the crowd sees it.

Bullish $NOK.

Estimated supplier revenue per $130k switch (based on BoM values):
NVIDIA (Switch ASICs + Optical Engines design): ~$12k (ASICs) + significant share of engines → major portion.

TSMC (Fabrication/Packaging of ASICs & engines): Substantial share of ~$44.4k combined ASIC/engines value.

Corning (FAUs + SM Fiber): ~$3.6k + $12.3k = ~$15.9k.

Lumentum / Coherent / Sumitomo (ELS + CW Lasers): ~$7.2k.

Additional (Shuffle Box, assembly): Balance to total BoM ~$75.8k.

Optical module packaging capacity is the quiet bottleneck behind AI network buildouts. A 10x lift at one plant, if it holds to the Q3 2026 timeline, moves the constraint elsewhere in the SiPho (silicon photonics) stack.

https://t.co/N8b2g3IDTM

Applied Energetics (OTCQB: AERG) CEO Chris Donaghey joined Morgan Stanley’s inaugural National Security Innovation Summit panel on the future of battlefield autonomy in New York.

Applied Energetics, Inc. (@AELaserTech)  (OTCQB: AERG), a developer of ultrashort pulse laser and directed energy technologies, took part in Morgan Stanley’s inaugural National Security Innovation Summit in New York on June 15, 2026, where President and CEO Chris Donaghey joined the panel “Machines in Motion: The Future of Battlefield Autonomy.”

Applied Energetics is a B2i Digital (@b2idigital) Featured Company. See the company’s profile at https://t.co/D4G4jB95ZV

The summit convened defense companies and institutional investors for a day of panels on national security technology, from autonomy and AI to space and missiles. On the battlefield-autonomy panel, Donaghey appeared alongside executives from Forterra, Firestorm Labs, and Lockheed Martin, in a discussion moderated by Morgan Stanley’s Kristine Liwag.

The themes ran close to Applied Energetics’ core work. According to the company, its ultrashort pulse laser portfolio targets counter-UAS, counter-sensor, infrared countermeasure, and other emerging directed energy missions, and aligns with the U.S. Department of War’s Critical Technology Area of Scaled Directed Energy, an area in sharper focus as militaries weigh how to counter low-cost drones at scale.

Visit the company’s website to learn more: https://t.co/Pd6aX43XBx

About Applied Energetics: Applied Energetics, Inc. specializes in advanced laser and photonics systems, particularly fiber-based ultrashort pulse (USP) laser technologies. With 26 patents and 2 patents pending, the company’s proprietary architecture is designed to enable size, weight, and power reductions relative to traditional continuous wave laser technology. AE’s dual-use systems target integration on defense platforms for high-intensity USP delivery, with applications across national security and commercial markets.

For investor information, contact Kevin McGrath, Managing Director, Cameron Associates, Inc., at kevin@cameronassoc.com or 646-418-7002. For additional information, visit https://t.co/VWa2WnbInK and https://t.co/4uzid7hTcd

Disclosure: David Shapiro, Chief Executive Officer of B2i Digital, personally purchased in the open market and currently owns shares of unrestricted AERG stock, in line with B2i Digital’s practice of investing alongside its Featured Companies. See the complete Disclosure at https://t.co/ljTVqQL4Si. B2i Digital is not a broker-dealer or investment adviser. This post is informational and not an offer or solicitation regarding any security.

text:
I want to address a common misconception  or maybe just ignorance I see on the part of numerous FinX influencers who clearly don’t understand the hedge fund business.  I don’t say that to be disparaging, but bc many of the comparisons I see are misleading and totally illogical really.   So if you see anyone do the following, take whatever they say with a  large grain of salt!

What I have seen are many individuals with what I would describe as unusually high returns this year going on and on about how they are outperforming this hedge fund and that hedge fund and how stupid people are to be paying large fees to those funds.  Now to be fair there are some crappy funds out there that don’t deserve the fees that they get, and that’s on the institutions that allocate large sums of capital without doing the proper due diligence.

But to compare one’s returns when one is running a 10 stock levered long portfolio of the usual AI/Optics/Space along with some https://t.co/Sy3Fyeq798 and $LPTH for defense tech exposure with those of Millennium, Citadel, Point 72, Tudor, etc is absurd.  Why?  Because the individuals going all in and margining w/ this stuff are only focused on return, they do not consider risk.  Why consider risk when everything is up and to the right?  

The top tier hedge funds are obsessed with risk management  and capital preservation.  They seek to avoid Cathie Wood style permanent capital destruction type investments at all costs.  You can laugh bc they are up 20% this year while you’re up 200%,  but that would be pretty foolish.  The “platform” hedge funds businsesses tend to run almost fully hedged long/short.  They don’t seek to ride beta, they seek to grind out 5-7% unlevered returns with little risk and then lever them 300-400%.  That is how they deliver all-weather performance over an entire business cycle.  Through the ups and the downs.  So when you are inevitably down 50%, they may be up 5-10% that year.  Beta reverts to the mean in time, alpha doesnt. 

The last time I saw these nonsensical comparisons was in 1999 when housewives became day-traders and often made fun of their hedge funds husbands who they were outperforming with stellar picks like VerticalNet, Nortel, Lucent, CMGI, and yes…even CIEN for DWDM.  This is CIEN’s second go around as a hot stock, most don’t know it was similarly en fuego in the late 90s just for different applications in fiber/telecom, not yet in photonics. 

That’s all.  Just wanted to make sure that when u see folks post crazy returns for  their concentrated high-risk and often margined portfolios, you realize that comparing them with the returns of the tried and true HF platforms makes little sense.  They have withstood the test of time, the influencer bragging about their returns has not.

Have a great long weekend everyone.  

JG

I don’t say that to be disparaging, but bc many of the comparisons I see are misleading and totally illogical", "id_str": "2067691686738976830", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 1, "reply_count": 12, "retweet_count": 8, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "1845105387608965146"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [{"indices": [993, 998], "text": "LPTH"}], "urls": [{"display_url": "EOS.AX", "expanded_url": "http://EOS.AX", "indices": [965, 988], "url": "https://t.co/Sy3Fyeq798"}], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNjc2OTE2ODY0NDU0Mjg3MzY=", "text": "I want to address a common misconception or maybe just ignorance I see on the part of numerous FinX influencers who clearly don’t understand the hedge fund business. I don’t say that to be disparaging, but bc many of the comparisons I see are misleading and totally illogical really. So if you see anyone do the following, take whatever they say with a large grain of salt! What I have seen are many individuals with what I would describe as unusually high returns this year going on and on about how they are outperforming this hedge fund and that hedge fund and how stupid people are to be paying large fees to those funds. Now to be fair there are some crappy funds out there that don’t deserve the fees that they get, and that’s on the institutions that allocate large sums of capital without doing the proper due diligence. But to compare one’s returns when one is running a 10 stock levered long portfolio of the usual AI/Optics/Space along with some https://t.co/Sy3Fyeq798 and $LPTH for defense tech exposure with those of Millennium, Citadel, Point 72, Tudor, etc is absurd. Why? Because the individuals going all in and margining w/ this stuff are only focused on return, they do not consider risk. Why consider risk when everything is up and to the right? The top tier hedge funds are obsessed with risk management and capital preservation. They seek to avoid Cathie Wood style permanent capital destruction type investments at all costs. You can laugh bc they are up 20% this year while you’re up 200%, but that would be pretty foolish. The “platform” hedge funds businsesses tend to run almost fully hedged long/short. They don’t seek to ride beta, they seek to grind out 5-7% unlevered returns with little risk and then lever them 300-400%. That is how they deliver all-weather performance over an entire business cycle. Through the ups and the downs. So when you are inevitably down 50%, they may be up 5-10% that year. Beta reverts to the mean in time, alpha doesnt. The last time I saw these nonsensical comparisons was in 1999 when housewives became day-traders and often made fun of their hedge funds husbands who they were outperforming with stellar picks like VerticalNet, Nortel, Lucent, CMGI, and yes…even CIEN for DWDM. This is CIEN’s second go around as a hot stock, most don’t know it was similarly en fuego in the late 90s just for different applications in fiber/telecom, not yet in photonics. That’s all. Just wanted to make sure that when u see folks post crazy returns for their concentrated high-risk and often margined portfolios, you realize that comparing them with the returns of the tried and true HF platforms makes little sense. They have withstood the test of time, the influencer bragging about their returns has not. Have a great long weekend everyone. Entries in the Global Photonics Engineering Contest 2026 will be judged on:

- Technical innovation and system-level thinking

- How effectively the solution addresses a real engineering challenge

- Clear and credible use of Photonic Chip technology

- Technical and commercial feasibility

- Quality of diagrams, schematics, and supporting visuals

The contest entries will be reviewed by a multidisciplinary jury panel of experts from PhotonDelta, ALUVIA Photonics, Bright Photonics, Epiphany-Design, imec, Ligentec, LioniX International, PHIX Photonics Assembly, SMART Photonics, XIVER MEMS Foundry, New Origin, PhotonVentures, and SBT Industries.

Learn more and submit your applications here: https://t.co/RxU4cUDzZe

text:
Aehr secures follow-on order for AI data center optical interconnect

Aehr Test Systems received a follow-on production order for a fully automated FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in (WLBI) system. This system, configured to test nine wafers in parallel, includes a WaferPak Auto Aligner and FOX WaferPak Contactors, with delivery expected within six months. The customer, a global leader in networking products, plans to use the system for advanced silicon photonics-based transceivers for hyperscale AI and cloud data centers. (https://t.co/8OBYA2YSGp)

Vik: Any guesses who this "customer" is? This is a "follow on" order mind you -- the first one was in April for $41M (https://t.co/H5J7MpXS5J). I'll just say Broadcom. If you disagree, leave a comment below.

@austinsemis @vikramskr

text:
Entegris $ENTG has quickly gone up 38% in just 7 days. 

It is now at 100x PE. I have not considered taking profit but maybe I will take some.

That being said, there is some recent good news for the business. 

> Entegris did a cross licensing deal with JSR and Inpria that ends a patent dispute they had over EUV photoresist and filtration technologies. Now, the companies will work together on the same matter. 

In my personal opinion, if you want exposure to the AI trade, then the lowest risk will come in the chemicals/materials and equipment side.

Many photonics names have quickly become overvalued. As long as the WFE market remains strong, the market of chemicals/materials will keep growing.

Here is my thesis: 

This is a well established business founded in 1966. 

Their products and solutions include: filtration products for gases & fluids, liquid systems, gas delivery systems, specialty chemistries for deposition and cleaning, wafer carriers and shippers, specialty coatings, premium graphite and silicon carbide, and shippers and trays for protecting and transporting disk drive components, and CMP slurries.

Basically a one stop shop for semiconductor companies, with ~94% of revenue related to semiconductor.

They report two segments: Materials Solutions (MS) and Advanced Purity Solutions (APS), but in the financial report for their 10-Q they break it down by:

- Semiconductor (fabs, equipment and engineering, chemical and materials, semi distributor and other)

- Non-semi

Think of MS as materials only and APS as filtration products.

The company controls roughly 35% to 40% of the global market for microcontamination control (liquid and gas filtration) and holds top-tier share positions in CMP (Chemical Mechanical Planarization) slurries, pads, and specialized wafer carriers. 

Additionally, Entegris and Pall Corporation (a subsidiary of Danaher) are the dominant duopoly leaders, controlling over 60% of the electronic filtration and purification market combined. Entegris specifically holds about 28% of the semiconductor liquid filter market. 

Why does this matter? 

As microchips continue to shrink, transitioning from 14nm nodes to 1.4nm, the margin for error shrinks alongside them. In fact, the allowable defect tolerance has plummeted tenfold, from 7nm to a mere 0.7nm. At this microscopic scale, even the slightest solid, liquid, or gaseous contamination can result in catastrophic financial damage. Entegris estimates that a mere 1% drop in production yield at the 1.4nm stage can drain up to $800 million annually from a fabrication facility. 

The company works hand-in-hand with its clients to design its purification systems directly into the end-to-end manufacturing systems. Because advanced chipmaking is so incredibly detailed, swapping out an established Entegris system for a rival product on an active production line is practically unfeasible which makes Entegris a long term success as long as chips keep getting more advanced. 

Per Q1'26: 

MS sales grew 2.8% YoY while APS sales grew 6.8% YoY. 

MS sales are ~43% of revenue; APS is ~57% 

They did suffer from sales decrease QoQ but that is mostly seasonality based (not a big deal).

Growth geographically is mixed for the most part, while sales of MS and APS are declining in North America, sales of APS significantly down in Japan...they are compensating this by growth in Taiwan for APS and MS.

The company's gross margin and operating margin have both increased YoY. 

The company is expected to finish FY26 at $3.63 EPS, a 134% increase YoY due to stronger AI demand. For FY27, it is estimated to be $4.65, dropping P/E from ~70x to 28x. 

Additional info:

They are also a confirmed supplier to SK Hynix for molybdenum NAND deposition. They supply Molybdenum Dichloride Dioxide (MoO₂Cl₂) which has seen adoption increase because it is cost effective, reduces resistivity in narrow features, and has an advantage over copper for interconnections by not needing a barrier layer.

Tungsten starts failing as NAND starts stacking above 300 layers, which is why molybdenum is seeing adoption.

Customers include:

Micron $MU
Intel $INTC
SK Hynix $000660.KS
Samsung $005930.KS
TSMC $TSM 
ASML $ASML 
Lam Research $LRCX
Tokyo Electron $803.5T
Applied Materials $AMAT

You can argue it is expensive right now, but there is nobody else that can benefit in this space like them and it is not a foreign company."}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2065108957933310219", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11332", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2067683962353225827", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/android" rel="nofollow">Twitter for Android</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3045", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Reality check:

• Fabrication paper, not a quantum result. No single-photon purity, no spin coherence, no entanglement.
• 82.9% is optical-coupling yield, not qubit yield.
• Coupling efficiency stays qualitative ("guided emission observed"), no dB number.
• Room temp, 6 nm lines, far from operating conditions.

But zoom out:

Manufacturability is exactly the unglamorous wall that has kept color-center quantum stuck at hero-device demos. Wafer-scale lithography at 82.9% yield is a real step out of that trap.

Direction: the diamond-photonics scaling story is shifting from "can we make one" to "can we make hundreds."

text:
Where this sits in the photonics-for-quantum arc:

• Caltech LEO comb, Cisco quantum switch, Harvard/NIST Yb-in-alumina
• Miraex/EPFL lithium-tantalate transduction
• This paper, diamond emitters lithographically married to alumina

The theme keeps compounding: integrated photonics is the picks-and-shovels layer under every modality.

“Neoclouds, Photonics, and Memory.

If you went long on software and chose the ideal stock, you’d probably end up not as happy? 

Photonics is still probably the earliest out of the three.

But I can see Nebius end up like AWS one day.”

Even got my warnings right too, $IREN is still stagnant due to the $6B of constant selling

text:
With #siliconphotonics being adopted across multiple layers of AI infrastructure — scale-up, scale-out, and scale-across — the cost comparison between #siliconphotonics and traditional discrete optical solutions is becoming much more relevant.
According to one analysis, silicon photonics offers a 2️⃣6️⃣% BOM advantage and 1️⃣5️⃣% price advantage for 8️⃣0️⃣0️⃣G. At 1️⃣6️⃣0️⃣0️⃣G, the advantage expands to 3️⃣2️⃣% on BOM and 2️⃣0️⃣% on price.
Interestingly, the biggest differential appears to be driven by lasers.
I believe for traditional discrete EML-based designs, the high-speed modulation is tied to the laser itself, so scaling bandwidth increases dependence on more expensive, high-performance EML components. In silicon photonics, the laser can increasingly act as a CW light source, while modulation and integration happen on the photonic IC. This changes the economics: fewer discrete optical components, higher integration, smaller form factor, lower power, and potentially better scalability as the industry moves from 800G to 1.6T and eventually 3.2T. 
That said, probabaly it isn ot a simple “SiPh replaces EML” story. Probably EML wouldcontinue to coexist in AI data centers, especially for longer-distance transmission due to its it is a mature, well-proven technology with a longer reliability track record. In AI clusters, network reliability is critical, and since GPUs remain the dominant cost driver, some architectures may view light-source cost savings as less relevant than proven performance and qualification history. #siliconphotonics provides an architectural shift by offering discrete optical components toward integration, packaging, thermal management, DSP/FEC, test, and system-level optimization. Let us see where silicon photonics wins and  where EML remains preferred, and how quickly AI infrastructure requirements embrases the architectural shift to more integrated optical solutions.

Directionally sound. Execution is the hard part. 👀

text:
⚡️ UPDATE: Photonics (watchlist)

1. Lasers/Transceivers/DSPs/PICs: $SIVE, $AAOI, $LITE, $MXL 
2. Full-stack compounders: $COHR, $CRDO 
3. Glass substrates: $LPK, $GLW 
4. Foundry: $XFAB, $TSEM 
5. PMA: $PENG 
6. Substrates: $AXTI, $SOI, $IQE 

Changes:

- Removed EMS - $JBL, $FLEX and $CLS - although they are great longs too. Prefer $CLS that is becoming an ODM (might add again in the future), or $JBL with their 1.6T program. 

- $UMC, $GFS don't only do SiPh and have run a lot. Would prefer $XFAB or $TSEM today for exposure to photonics. 

- Added $MXL and $CRDO that look like serious players here. $ALAB, not yet.

1. Lasers/Transceivers:

- $LITE, $AAOI (CW, EML lasers & transceivers)

2. Substrates: 
- $SOI (SOI-substrates), 
- $AXTI (InP & GaAs substrates), 
- $IQE (Epitaxy), 
- $GLW, $LPK (Glass) https://t.co/ixUCdDJ6p3

text:
💥 $JABIL Infrastructure news💥
​Adani & Jabil just announced a massive strategic alliance to build a multi-GW vertically integrated AI data center manufacturing platform in India 🇮🇳 targeting a $3T market.
​This is huge for the Physical AI stack and our baby $SIVE among others.

​- Massive Scale Integration: They aren't just building boards. They are building full-stack liquid-cooled AI racks, high-density servers, and networking systems, alongside full "grey space" infrastructure (CDUs, PDUs, and power switchgears).

​-Captive Hyper-Scale Pipeline: This connects directly into Adani’s $100 Billion commitment to deploy 5 GW of green-energy-powered hyperscale data centers by 2035. Jabil effectively captures an unprecedented, long-term sovereign hardware buyer.

​- The Silicon Photonics Read-Through: When you scale data centers at a multi-gigawatt level, copper architecture physically bottlenecks under the sheer compute density. To make these liquid-cooled multi-GW hubs functional, hyperscalers will be structurally forced to deploy next-gen optical interconnects and Silicon Photonics from day one.

​The physical hardware footprint for AI compute is expanding globally, massively expanding the total addressable market (TAM) for pick-and-shovel component suppliers.  #SiliconPhotonics #AI #Hardware #Tech

text:
The paper identifies specific physical and engineering constraints in current hardware as a meaningful friction:

"Even if economic resources and raw FLOPs can continue to be scaled, physical and engineering constraints in current hardware architectures may pose significant challenges. Specifically, memory bandwidth limits and interconnect bottlenecks (the difficulty of scaling high-speed communication between thousands of chips) can severely limit effective compute utilization. As models grow, the time spent moving data between memory and compute units, or between different compute nodes, can become the dominant factor, leading to diminishing returns in scaling unless novel hardware paradigms or interconnect technologies are developed."

This is one of the reasons why silicon photonics, and the idea of improving networking becomes significantly important moving ahead, and why names like $LITE and $COHR are going to be a lot more prominent.

This is a must-see. The paper is explicit that if current trends hold through the end of the decade, the implications are staggering: "The pace that AI development has picked up over the past decade might continue without major blockers until at least the end of this decade, which would, e.g., imply growth in effective compute by a factor of 10,000 compared to today." It also gives a concrete illustrative scenario for what scaling AGI instances alone might look like: "Suppose that by the time human-level AGI is available, base model progress plateaus but effective compute continues to grow at 10× for a bit longer. Even if AGI were initially expensive to run, and only 1000 instances could be run, after a year it would be 10,000, and after five years it would be 100 million instances; or 1 million instances a hundred times faster. Would this form of scaling give us ASI? If not after 5 years, what about 10 years, or 15?" On population scaling of AI instances specifically, the paper references back-of-the-envelope estimates of about 25× per year in AI "population scaling." The paper explicitly mentions large-scale physical infrastructure as both a prerequisite and a bottleneck, flagging "investments into energy production and Gigawatt AI infrastructure" as lending credibility to continued scaling trajectories. Our strategy must be smarter. Our true competitive advantage is our massive digital market with ~13 Billion Monthly UPI Transactions processing ~$240 Billion USD.
​This frictionless economy generates behavioral data of unprecedented quality. This data is our bargaining chip in the new negotiation technology landscape. Our goal is not a self-sufficient ecosystem, but an irreplaceable one in the global value chain.
Strategic Alliances are Key: We must stop relying on the west and build a warehouse of specialized non-US allies.
​🔗 Japan: Optical Computing & Advanced Photonics.
🔗 South Korea: High-Density Data Center Comms & Power Highways.
🔗 Taiwan: Advanced Chip Manufacturing.
🔗 Europe (e.g., Swiss/Netherlands): High-Precision Machines.

text:
The quantum race is entering a new phase.

QuEra’s roadmap for a fault-tolerant neutral-atom quantum computer by 2028 isn’t just another product announcement—it’s another signal that the industry is shifting from scientific experiments toward commercially useful systems.

What’s becoming increasingly interesting is the convergence of the ecosystem:

• AWS is expanding access through Amazon Braket.
• Google is investing in neutral-atom technology.
• QuEra, Atom Computing, Pasqal, and other leaders continue pushing scalability and error correction.
• Private capital and enterprise demand are accelerating development.

For investors, the biggest opportunity may not come from a single winner. Quantum computing will require an entire supply chain—cryogenics, lasers, photonics, advanced semiconductors, networking, cloud infrastructure, AI software, and specialized materials.

The companies that enable fault tolerance, lower error rates, and scalable deployment could become the Nvidia-equivalent infrastructure providers of the quantum era.

The technology is still early, but the milestones are becoming more frequent, the capital commitments are getting larger, and the path toward commercial quantum computing is looking increasingly credible. $GOOG

text:
The main point:

I do not think the AI trade ends with GPUs.

The next layer is physical infrastructure.

Optics.
Lasers.
Substrates.
Wafers.
Power semis.
Photonics foundries.
Specialized equipment.
That is where I think Europe has some of the most mispriced AI supply-chain names.

text:
🚨🚨🚨📢

$SPX $SPY $QQQ $IWM $VIX

While everyone is focused on Trump playing geopolitical ping pong with Iran, I'm watching AI and semiconductors inflate what may become the mother of all bubbles.

The real story isn't today's headlines.

It's the $700B to $750B annual AI infrastructure arms race quietly unfolding beneath the surface. 👇

1/
The hyperscalers, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, are spending like drunken sailors with unlimited credit cards.

Annual AI CapEx is projected to reach $700B to $750B.

Global AI related debt issuance could exceed $500B.

They're borrowing against the future and praying the future arrives on schedule.

2/
The biggest risk?

Stranded capital.

Today they're borrowing billions to fill data centers with power hungry Nvidia GPUs.

The problem is those assets are depreciated over 5 to 6 years.

Technology may make them obsolete in 2 to 3.

That's not innovation.

That's a future write off waiting for an earnings call.

3/
Imagine owing bondholders hundreds of billions while sitting on warehouses full of yesterday's hardware.

Silicon photonics.

Neuromorphic computing.

Custom ASICs.

If these technologies arrive faster than expected, today's AI infrastructure could become the corporate equivalent of buying a fleet of steam engines right before electricity.

4/
The spending frenzy also reveals another problem.

Free cash flow.

CapEx is consuming an extraordinary share of revenue:

• Alphabet about 46%
• Microsoft about 47%
• Meta about 54%

The more they spend, the more cash flow gets squeezed.

They're running faster just to stay in place.

5/
This is why everyone wants their own chips.

Google TPUs.

Amazon Trainium.

Meta custom silicon.

The goal isn't just performance.

The goal is survival.

They need lower operating costs to justify the debt they're piling onto their balance sheets today.

6/
Then comes the next twist.

If AI chips become dramatically more efficient, power stops being the bottleneck.

Location becomes the advantage.

Data centers may no longer need to sit next to massive power sources.

They can move closer to population centers, reducing latency and potentially turning today's expensive power agreements into tomorrow's capital allocation mistakes.

7/
And then there's the risk nobody wants to discuss.

The Inference Slump.

The entire AI spending boom assumes enterprise demand explodes.

But many AI pilot programs still struggle to generate meaningful returns.

If AI inference becomes extremely cheap, compute becomes a commodity.

And commodities don't generate bubble valuations.

8/
That's the paradox.

The hyperscalers need hyper efficient chips to save themselves.

But those same chips could eventually destroy the economics supporting today's AI infrastructure boom.

The cure may become the disease.

9/
Ultimately, Big Tech is engaged in a historic game of financial chicken.

They're borrowing hundreds of billions today.

Betting that tomorrow's technology arrives before today's debt becomes a problem.

Wall Street calls it innovation.

History often calls it a bubble.

Choose carefully which chapter we're currently writing.

text:
Bir başka diğer bahsetmek istediğim şirket ise ;

$COHR 

Anlık Fiyat :389 #dolar

COHR Tam Olarak Ne İş Yapıyor?

Coherent Corp.

Coherent, dünyanın en büyük fotonik (ışık teknolojileri) şirketlerinden biridir.

Basit anlatırsak:

NVDA hesap yapıyor.✅

 MU veriyi depoluyor.✅

 COHR ise bu verinin veri merkezinde ışıkla taşınmasını sağlıyor. ✅

Yani yapay zekanın sinir sistemi diyebileceğimiz katmanda yer alıyor. (İşte en can alıcı nokta bu )

Şirketin Ana İş Kolları : 

1. Optik Transceiver'lar

Bunlar veri merkezlerinde kullanılan özel modüller.

Görevleri:

GPU → GPU
Sunucu → Sunucu
Rack → Rack

arasındaki veriyi ışıkla taşımak.

AI modelleri büyüdükçe bu ürünlerin önemi katlanıyor. ⚡️⚡️

2. Silicon Photonics

Bence şirketin en kritik alanı.

Elektrik sinyallerini ışığa dönüştürüyor.

Avantajları:

Daha az enerji tüketimi✅

Daha yüksek hız✅

Daha düşük gecikme✅

Daha az ısı✅

NVIDIA'nın gelecek nesil veri merkezi mimarisinde tam olarak ihtiyaç duyulan teknoloji bu.

3. Lazerler

COHR dünyanın en büyük endüstriyel ve telekom lazer üreticilerinden biri.

Ürettikleri:

InP (Indium Phosphide) lazerler
CW lazerler
VCSEL lazerler
Telekom lazerleri

Bu lazerler hem kendi ürünlerinde hem de sektörün diğer oyuncularında kullanılıyor.

4. Co-Packaged Optics (CPO)

Şu anda Wall Street'in en heyecanlandığı konu.

Bugün:

GPU ↔ Optik modül ayrı.

Yarın:

GPU ↔ Optik modül aynı pakette olacak.

Bu teknolojiye CPO deniyor.

NVIDIA'nın gelecekteki veri merkezi planlarının merkezinde bu teknoloji var. 

COHR da burada ana tedarikçilerden biri olmaya çalışıyor.

Kimlerle Çalışıyor?

Kesin müşteri listeleri her zaman açıklanmıyor ancak sektör analizlerinde ve şirket sunumlarında şu isimler sürekli karşımıza çıkıyor:

NVIDIA
Microsoft Azure
Amazon Web Services
Meta
Google Cloud

Çünkü bunların hepsi devasa AI veri merkezleri kuruyor ve optik ağ altyapısına ihtiyaç duyuyorlar.

NVIDIA ile İlişkisi

Burası çok önemli.

2026'da $NVDA :

Coherent'e yaklaşık 2 milyar dolar yatırım yaptı.

Uzun vadeli satın alma anlaşmaları yaptı.

Lazer ve optik bileşen kapasitesini garanti altına aldı.

Bu haber çıktığında COHR hissesi sert yükselmişti.

Çünkü bu durum:

"NVIDIA gelecekte fotoniğin kritik olacağını düşünüyor."

mesajını verdi.

Neden Yapay Zeka İçin Kritik?

Bugün darboğaz:

GPU sayısı değil.

GPU'ların birbirleriyle haberleşmesi.

Rakipleri Kimler?

En büyük rakipler:

Lumentum Holdings $LITE 

Applied Optoelectronics $AAOI 

Corning $GLW 

Broadcom $AVGO 

Marvell Technology $MRVL

Ama COHR'nin farkı:

Lazer + Transceiver + Silicon Photonics + CPO bileşenlerini aynı çatı altında sunabilmesi.

Coherent Corp. Coherent, dünyanın en büyük fotonik (ışık teknolojileri) şirketlerinden biridir. Basit anlatırsak: NVDA hesap yapıyor.✅ MU veriyi depoluyor.✅ COHR ise bu verinin veri merkezinde ışıkla taşınmasını sağlıyor. ✅ Yani yapay zekanın sinir sistemi diyebileceğimiz katmanda yer alıyor. (İşte en can alıcı nokta bu ) Şirketin Ana İş Kolları : 1. Optik Transceiver'lar Bunlar veri merkezlerinde kullanılan özel modüller. Görevleri: GPU → GPU Sunucu → Sunucu Rack → Rack arasındaki veriyi ışıkla taşımak. AI modelleri büyüdükçe bu ürünlerin önemi katlanıyor. ⚡️⚡️ 2. Silicon Photonics Bence şirketin en kritik alanı. Elektrik sinyallerini ışığa dönüştürüyor. Avantajları: Daha az enerji tüketimi✅ Daha yüksek hız✅ Daha düşük gecikme✅ Daha az ısı✅ NVIDIA'nın gelecek nesil veri merkezi mimarisinde tam olarak ihtiyaç duyulan teknoloji bu. 3. Lazerler COHR dünyanın en büyük endüstriyel ve telekom lazer üreticilerinden biri. Ürettikleri: InP (Indium Phosphide) lazerler CW lazerler VCSEL lazerler Telekom lazerleri Bu lazerler hem kendi ürünlerinde hem de sektörün diğer oyuncularında kullanılıyor. 4. Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) Şu anda Wall Street'in en heyecanlandığı konu. Bugün: GPU ↔ Optik modül ayrı. Yarın: GPU ↔ Optik modül aynı pakette olacak. Bu teknolojiye CPO deniyor. NVIDIA'nın gelecekteki veri merkezi planlarının merkezinde bu teknoloji var. COHR da burada ana tedarikçilerden biri olmaya çalışıyor. Kimlerle Çalışıyor? Kesin müşteri listeleri her zaman açıklanmıyor ancak sektör analizlerinde ve şirket sunumlarında şu isimler sürekli karşımıza çıkıyor: NVIDIA Microsoft Azure Amazon Web Services Meta Google Cloud Çünkü bunların hepsi devasa AI veri merkezleri kuruyor ve optik ağ altyapısına ihtiyaç duyuyorlar. NVIDIA ile İlişkisi Burası çok önemli. 2026'da $NVDA : Coherent'e yaklaşık 2 milyar dolar yatırım yaptı. Uzun vadeli satın alma anlaşmaları yaptı. Lazer ve optik bileşen kapasitesini garanti altına aldı. Bu haber çıktığında COHR hissesi sert yükselmişti. Çünkü bu durum: "NVIDIA gelecekte fotoniğin kritik olacağını düşünüyor." mesajını verdi. Neden Yapay Zeka İçin Kritik? Bugün darboğaz: GPU sayısı değil. GPU'ların birbirleriyle haberleşmesi. Rakipleri Kimler? En büyük rakipler: Lumentum Holdings $LITE Applied Optoelectronics $AAOI Corning $GLW Broadcom $AVGO Marvell Technology $MRVL Ama COHR'nin farkı: Lazer + Transceiver + Silicon Photonics + CPO bileşenlerini aynı çatı altında sunabilmesi."}}}, "rest_id": "2068380860089553357", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1280", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
This week I learned the photonics trade is finally shifting from vibes to contracts.

$AXTI got a RMB173M / USD25.4M InP wafer deal with prepay/minimum-buy language. IQE got Tower commitments. AEHR got a follow-on SiPh test order.

Still tiny-scout territory. Proof is not size.
Go $GLW guys if you want to participate in the Photonics scale-up 😉

text:
The 5 best stocks and ETFs to own right now: 👇

$DRAM - memory ETF that includes $SNDK, $MU, Samsung and more. This is a growing space and will only continue to grow.

$TSLA - high and volatile gas prices are increasing demand for EVs. Energy storage systems, charging stations and autonomous capabilities are growing immensely. Robots are coming soon.

$SOFI - with oil stabilizing over time, the chances for rate cuts due to lower inflation is higher. This company is highly profitable and is scaling up quickly. They’re projecting 40% growth over the next 3-4 years.

$AAOI - photonics is a huge market and is growing in demand as AI data centers scale. These guys are positioned well to crush it over the next 5+ years. 

$ONDS - there has been a huge increase in the demand of autonomous drones, boats and on land vehicles used in military operations across the globe. Ondas has all the right products and services the U.S. military and other allied nations would need. 

What 5 stocks would you choose? 🤔

text:
Why i bought $aeva at $14 in May 2026 and Why i will buy it again at $24

The legacy LiDAR market is heavily saturated with companies building systems that are essentially just rapid 3D cameras. Aeva Technologies ($AEVA) is completely rewriting this architectural standard. By transitioning from traditional time-of-flight sensors to Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) technology, Aeva is moving machine perception from static imagery to real-time 4D understanding.
Here is exactly why $AEVA is positioned as a foundational deep-tech asset as we approach the next wave of autonomous infrastructure:
  Traditional LiDAR simply measures the time it takes for a laser pulse to return, providing basic distance data. Aeva’s FMCW continuous modulation captures not just depth and reflectivity, but per-point instant velocity at the pixel level. This means the sensor instantly knows exactly how fast an object is moving in real-time. This precision is non-negotiable for Level 3 and Level 4 autonomous driving, where high-speed decision-making is required.
  The most significant structural validation for Aeva recently occurred when its 4D LiDAR was selected for the NVIDIA DRIVE Hyperion autonomous vehicle reference platform. Aeva and NVIDIA will collaborate to integrate this perception platform into Hyperion to support production vehicle programs targeted in 2028. Furthermore, Aeva has already secured an exclusive LiDAR supplier role (outside China) for a top European passenger vehicle OEM, with production also targeted for 2028.
  Aeva is rapidly expanding beyond civilian applications and directly into the highly lucrative defense sector. In Q1 2026, the CEO highlighted that Aeva’s technology is being integrated into a second autonomous ground vehicle through an expansion with Forterra. A massive tactical advantage of Aeva's specific hardware is its wavelength undetectability by night vision systems, opening up immediate expansion opportunities for military drones and ground operations.
  While many hardware startups struggle with cash burn, Aeva is showing definitive commercial momentum. In Q1 2026, the company reported a record revenue of $6.3 million, marking a 90% year-over-year increase. While the company still operates at a net loss of nearly $35 million, this revenue surge indicates a successful scaling transition from laboratory testing to securing major customer development milestones.
Aeva is no longer just pitching a whitepaper; their silicon-photonics hardware is actively being baked into the global automotive architecture of the future.
$AEVA #LiDAR #AutonomousVehicles #DeepTech #FMCW #NvidiaDrive

Aeva Technologies ($AEVA) is completely rewriting this architectural standard. By transitioning from traditional time-of-flight sensors to Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) technology, Aeva is moving machine perception from static imagery to real-time 4D understanding. Here is exactly why $AEVA is positioned as a foundational deep-tech asset as we approach the next wave of autonomous infrastructure: Traditional LiDAR simply measures the time it takes for a laser pulse to return, providing basic distance data. Aeva’s FMCW continuous modulation captures not just depth and reflectivity, but per-point instant velocity at the pixel level. This means the sensor instantly knows exactly how fast an object is moving in real-time. This precision is non-negotiable for Level 3 and Level 4 autonomous driving, where high-speed decision-making is required. The most significant structural validation for Aeva recently occurred when its 4D LiDAR was selected for the NVIDIA DRIVE Hyperion autonomous vehicle reference platform. Aeva and NVIDIA will collaborate to integrate this perception platform into Hyperion to support production vehicle programs targeted in 2028. Furthermore, Aeva has already secured an exclusive LiDAR supplier role (outside China) for a top European passenger vehicle OEM, with production also targeted for 2028. Aeva is rapidly expanding beyond civilian applications and directly into the highly lucrative defense sector. In Q1 2026, the CEO highlighted that Aeva’s technology is being integrated into a second autonomous ground vehicle through an expansion with Forterra. A massive tactical advantage of Aeva's specific hardware is its wavelength undetectability by night vision systems, opening up immediate expansion opportunities for military drones and ground operations. While many hardware startups struggle with cash burn, Aeva is showing definitive commercial momentum. In Q1 2026, the company reported a record revenue of $6.3 million, marking a 90% year-over-year increase. While the company still operates at a net loss of nearly $35 million, this revenue surge indicates a successful scaling transition from laboratory testing to securing major customer development milestones. Aeva is no longer just pitching a whitepaper; their silicon-photonics hardware is actively being baked into the global automotive architecture of the future. $AEVA #LiDAR #AutonomousVehicles #DeepTech #FMCW #NvidiaDrive"}}}, "rest_id": "2068770017777524798", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "416", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Market's reaction to $NOK is crazy

This is what happened recently: 
- $NVDA purchasing stake 
- $4B US manufacturing push. 
- AI & Cloud customer sales +49% in Q1
- Insiders purchased stock at higher than current price

And still stock fell this week after the 2x YTD run into mid-June. Nokia is now a credible photonics & AI-networking supplier that demands re-rating. 

👀I'm watching one thing: Growth in AI & cloud customers. The whole thesis lives there.

Bought the NVIDIA-Nokia headline and feel unhappy? Personal Opinion : Hold and wait, better days are waiting .  

What's your cost basis ?

NFA. DYOR. Hold long position

text:
I'm warning you now.

If hyperscalers do double current issuance expectations, these are the stocks to buy:

Highest alignment are core data-center/ interconnect/memory/capacity plays (listed below).

$SIVE, $IQE, $AAOI, $OPTX: Photonics/optics enablers (lasers, epi-wafers, transceivers, custom optics). Critical for scale-out networking and CPO in larger AI clusters. Higher hyperscaler spend = more fiber/interconnect demand. 

$MU: HBM memory. Hyperscalers are the primary buyers; supply is already sold out for 2026 with multi-year agreements. Accelerated capex reinforces the supercycle.

$IREN, $BRUN, $CRWV, $HIVE, $CLSK: AI cloud/HPC/GPU capacity and power infrastructure providers. Hyperscalers buy capacity or compete in the buildout; more spend supports power and compute demand.

$ASYS, $ICHR, $INTT: Semiconductor equipment/subsystems/test for AI GPU packaging and chip production ramp. Direct beneficiary of higher chip volumes needed for hyperscaler clusters.

$PENG: Advanced computing + memory solutions for AI/HPC deployments.

Moderate/edge alignment:

$AMPG: RF/microwave amplifiers and 5G/O-RAN subsystems. Benefits from broader AI infrastructure (AI-RAN, edge wireless, satellite for distributed setups) and new customers like NVIDIA/Amazon. Less direct than pure DC enablers but positive from overall spend.

$SILC, $NOK: Networking/telecom infrastructure supporting AI data flows and edge.

$XFAB: Specialty foundry for power/analog/sensors used in AI systems.

Lower or indirect:

$MCHP, $MX, $AMBQ: More diversified/edge-focused; some benefit but less concentrated exposure.

$AKAM: Edge delivery; indirect tailwind from overall AI growth.

I'm really sticking to the main high alignment plays (that I've been saying for a bit).

$OPTX, $ASYS, $IQE, $HIVE $BRUN in particular are the plays I like.

Buy any of the good ones listed above, and I believe you will perform great over 2026-2027.

Just my 2c on what happens next.

Any other good plays that I'm missing here?

text: "Does AMD do it? Yes. AMD acquired Enosemi (a silicon-photonics startup) in May 2025 to build CPO/optical-I/O for its future CPUs/GPUs, and it's an Ayar Labs investor. So AMD is in the race for its MI-series "Helios" rack-scale systems — but a clear step behind Nvidia on timing and scale."

text: "Google is actually ahead of Nvidia here: TPU pods have used optical circuit switching (OCS) at the pod level since TPU v4. So the hypers were optical-interconnect pioneers; CPO just brings the optics on-package. They don't build the photonics themselves — they get it through their co-design partner (Broadcom/Marvell) + merchant photonics + TSMC packaging."

text:
While everyone is focused on Trump playing geopolitical ping pong with Iran, I'm watching AI and semiconductors inflate what may become the mother of all bubbles.

The real story isn't today's headlines.

It's the $700B to $750B annual AI infrastructure arms race quietly unfolding beneath the surface. 👇

1/
The hyperscalers, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, are spending like drunken sailors with unlimited credit cards.

Annual AI CapEx is projected to reach $700B to $750B.

Global AI related debt issuance could exceed $500B.

They're borrowing against the future and praying the future arrives on schedule.

2/
The biggest risk?

Stranded capital.

Today they're borrowing billions to fill data centers with power hungry Nvidia GPUs.

The problem is those assets are depreciated over 5 to 6 years.

Technology may make them obsolete in 2 to 3.

That's not innovation.

That's a future write off waiting for an earnings call.

3/
Imagine owing bondholders hundreds of billions while sitting on warehouses full of yesterday's hardware.

Silicon photonics.

Neuromorphic computing.

Custom ASICs.

If these technologies arrive faster than expected, today's AI infrastructure could become the corporate equivalent of buying a fleet of steam engines right before electricity.

4/
The spending frenzy also reveals another problem.

Free cash flow.

CapEx is consuming an extraordinary share of revenue:

• Alphabet about 46%
• Microsoft about 47%
• Meta about 54%

The more they spend, the more cash flow gets squeezed.

They're running faster just to stay in place.

5/
This is why everyone wants their own chips.

Google TPUs.

Amazon Trainium.

Meta custom silicon.

The goal isn't just performance.

The goal is survival.

They need lower operating costs to justify the debt they're piling onto their balance sheets today.

6/
Then comes the next twist.

If AI chips become dramatically more efficient, power stops being the bottleneck.

Location becomes the advantage.

Data centers may no longer need to sit next to massive power sources.

They can move closer to population centers, reducing latency and potentially turning today's expensive power agreements into tomorrow's capital allocation mistakes.

7/
And then there's the risk nobody wants to discuss.

The Inference Slump.

The entire AI spending boom assumes enterprise demand explodes.

But many AI pilot programs still struggle to generate meaningful returns.

If AI inference becomes extremely cheap, compute becomes a commodity.

And commodities don't generate bubble valuations.

8/
That's the paradox.

The hyperscalers need hyper efficient chips to save themselves.

But those same chips could eventually destroy the economics supporting today's AI infrastructure boom.

The cure may become the disease.

9/
Ultimately, Big Tech is engaged in a historic game of financial chicken.

They're borrowing hundreds of billions today.

Betting that tomorrow's technology arrives before today's debt becomes a problem.

Wall Street calls it innovation.

History often calls it a bubble.

Choose carefully which chapter we're currently writing.

text:
$QQQ 
At ~$660, within a 52-week range of $402 to $688. The Nasdaq was the week’s clear standout, jumping 2.4% as the chip complex caught fire on a landmark deal. President Trump announced that Intel would design and build chips domestically alongside Apple, sending Intel surging 11.45% Thursday and dragging the entire semiconductor group higher. It was a broad AI and chip melt-up: SanDisk climbed 11.63%, Super Micro added 10.10%, and Enphase jumped 10% on production shipments of its IQ9S microinverters plus a Barclays upgrade. Data-center and photonics names followed, with Corning up 7%, Credo up 5%, and Coherent up 4%. The rally is notable because it came in the same week the Fed turned hawkish and Treasury yields spiked, conditions that usually punish long-duration tech. Instead, the Intel-Apple news and the peace deal overwhelmed the rate fear. SpaceX, fresh off last week’s record $1.77 trillion IPO, anchors Saylor’s new “Magnificent 8” framing. The one persistent worry remains concentration: a handful of AI-linked megacaps still account for nearly 40% of the entire index, so the Nasdaq’s fate is tied to whether this chip enthusiasm holds through Micron’s earnings next week.

The Nasdaq shrugged off a hawkish Fed and surging yields to rip 2.4%, led by Intel up 11.45% on the Apple chip deal. When tech rallies through a yield spike, the AI narrative is doing the heavy lifting. Micron earnings next week are the test of whether it is justified.

text:
A lot of the AI infrastructure thesis from the past few weeks are starting to play out.

Neocloud / Sovereign AI:
$CRWV 100 → 121
$NBIS 228 → 297

Memory:
Still holding dram long from ~68, added more $SKHYNIX
$MU earnings next week could be another important read-through for HBM demand. (Jun 24)

Photonics:

China just approved the first major 2026 InP substrate export batch, providing some near-term relief to one of the optical bottlenecks discussed in my photonics thread.

I’m still watching this closely. Demand for 800G/1.6T AI interconnects continues to explode, so a single export batch doesn’t solve the structural shortage, it simply unlocks some capacity. The real question is whether supply can keep up with AI cluster buildouts over the next 12–24 months.

Meanwhile:
• HBM4E sampling next month
• SK Hynix US ADR listing
• SK Hynix earnings (Jul 23)
• Ongoing sovereign AI buildouts globally

The AI trade keeps moving up the stack.

First GPUs.
Then neoclouds.
Then photonics.
Now memory.

The biggest winners continue to come from identifying the bottlenecks before the market fully prices them in.

text:
🇺🇸 | نتائج توصيات السوق الأمريكي اليوم

يوم جديد يؤكد أن قوة التداول ليست في كثرة الصفقات، بل في دقة الاختيار وإدارة الخروج.

تم اليوم إغلاق صفقتين على أرباح قوية:

✅سهم Laser Photonics LASE
من 2.38 إلى 2.65
ربح محقق: +11.3%

✅سهم Roblox RBLX
من 43 إلى 49.13
ربح محقق: +14.3%

وفي المقابل، تم إغلاق صفقة ResMed RMD عند وقف الخسارة بخسارة محدودة تقارب -2%، وهذا جزء طبيعي من أي خطة تداول منضبطة.

📍الخلاصة:
نترك الأرباح تكبر عندما تسير الصفقة في صالحنا، ونغلق الخسارة مبكرًا عندما لا تؤكد الحركة توقعاتنا.

💥هكذا تُبنى النتائج:
ربح قوي عند تحقق الأهداف، وخسارة محدودة عند فشل السيناريو.

 📍في Stock Basket، نعتمد على الدمج بين إدارة الصفقة باحتراف، والبوت الذكي الذي يساعدنا في فرز وتصنيف الفرص داخل السوق قبل التنفيذ.

👈 تفضل واحجز مكانك لمتابعة صفقات واضحة بمستويات دخول، أهداف، حماية ربح، وإدارة دقيقة أثناء الجلسة، 
-الانضمام للمجموعة الذهبية هو الخطوة الأقرب لك.

📩 للاشتراك والتفاصيل، راسلنا الآن.

⚠️ إخلاء مسؤولية:
جميع المعلومات لأغراض إعلامية فقط وليست توصية مباشرة. يُرجى الرجوع إلى مستشار مالي مرخّص قبل أي قرار.
